    "1c7c1fd401db7adddee473fe9ab7759cc27bfe50d707af6584fa7095181c9c8a": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495082187,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/austin_hurwitz/status/2023726021858783330",
          "id": "2023726021858783330",
          "text": "",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "https://t.co/2i3n2HWYGX",
            "facets": []
          },
          "author": {
            "id": "306521093",
            "name": "austin",
            "screen_name": "austin_hurwitz",
            "avatar_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2002729532919345152/l2dbbgCa_200x200.jpg",
            "banner_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_banners/306521093/1768171419",
            "description": "agentic media systems @neukoai | IP $GBOY | agent token infra @blowfishbot | making cool shit online with my friends",
            "location": "NYC",
            "url": "https://x.com/austin_hurwitz",
            "followers": 23374,
            "following": 2882,
            "joined": "Sat May 28 01:11:49 +0000 2011",
            "likes": 55510,
            "media_count": 2230,
            "protected": false,
            "website": {
              "url": "http://gboyspecial.com",
              "display_url": "gboyspecial.com"
            },
            "tweets": 20547,
            "avatar_color": null
          },
          "replies": 30,
          "retweets": 47,
          "likes": 438,
          "bookmarks": 1245,
          "created_at": "Tue Feb 17 11:47:37 +0000 2026",
          "created_timestamp": 1771328857,
          "possibly_sensitive": false,
          "views": 36470,
          "is_note_tweet": false,
          "community_note": null,
          "lang": null,
          "replying_to": null,
          "replying_to_status": null,
          "source": "Twitter Web App",
          "article": {
            "created_at": "2026-02-17T11:47:37.000Z",
            "modified_at": "2026-02-17T11:47:37.000Z",
            "id": "2023721866456698880",
            "title": "Give Your OpenClaw Agent Memory that Actually Works",
            "preview_text": "Everyone's experience with OpenClaw starts the same. It feels like this great unlock - an agent who has context on everything you do and can take actions on your behalf.\nThen inevitably it hits a",
            "cover_media": {
              "id": "QVBJTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHBW37GubYAAKAAIAAAAAEkUkBQAA",
              "media_id": "2023725833618415616",
              "media_info": {
                "__typename": "ApiImage",
                "color_info": {
                  "palette": [
                    {
                      "percentage": 47.63,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 51,
                        "green": 45,
                        "red": 2
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 8.78,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 30,
                        "green": 89,
                        "red": 80
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 7.75,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 161,
                        "green": 161,
                        "red": 201
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 4.76,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 51,
                        "green": 54,
                        "red": 71
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 4.72,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 45,
                        "green": 123,
                        "red": 58
                      }
                    }
                  ]
                },
                "original_img_height": 550,
                "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HBW37GubYAA1uDb.jpg",
                "original_img_width": 1376
              },
              "media_key": "3_2023725833618415616"
            },
            "content": {
              "blocks": [
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "8lbu1",
                  "text": "Everyone's experience with OpenClaw starts the same. It feels like this great unlock - an agent who has context on everything you do and can take actions on your behalf.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fh9n",
                  "text": "Then inevitably it hits a wall.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3lggc",
                  "text": " \"I'm sorry, I don't remember what we were doing\"",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "el0gg",
                  "text": "Memory is the biggest problem to solve and while my setup isn't fool proof it will get you in a much better place than your out of the box solution. ",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 50,
                        "text": "https://github.com/tobi/qmd",
                        "toIndex": 77
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fbet4",
                  "text": "Step 1: Install QMD and make it the default (link:https://github.com/tobi/qmd)",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fh0oj",
                  "text": "Think of QMD as an on device search engine which indexes your various markdown files for more reliable, consistent, queries. ",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 72,
                        "text": "Tools.md",
                        "toIndex": 80
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "cle9l",
                  "text": "Tell your agent to always default to QMD for search and save it in it's Tools.md file. ",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "2al7m",
                  "text": "Step 2: Split up memory into four files: long term memory,  daily notes, active tasks, and lessons. ",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "dctjo",
                  "text": "The default memory system crams everything into a single markdown file. Over time this can become increasingly difficult for your agent to parse. Breaking the system into four files across the lifecycle of an agent's memory gives it more targeted searches depending on the context. When the agent makes a mistake be sure it always logs it in it's lessons file. ",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "d28ri",
                  "text": "Step 3: Maintenance",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "2nfmt",
                  "text": "Setup periodic crons as hygiene for your files. ",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 65,
                        "text": "tools.md",
                        "toIndex": 73
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4phmc",
                  "text": "Review memory for anything that's actually a tool and move it to tools.md",
                  "type": "ordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 48,
                        "text": "lessons.md",
                        "toIndex": 58
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4b7o2",
                  "text": "Move key lessons from daily notes and memory to lessons.md ",
                  "type": "ordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "bq18g",
                  "text": "Remove outdated context ",
                  "type": "ordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "6s3f3",
                  "text": "Memory System Skill File (Point Your Agent Here)",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [
                    {
                      "key": 0,
                      "length": 1,
                      "offset": 0
                    }
                  ],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "2jpnl",
                  "text": " ",
                  "type": "atomic"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "611je",
                  "text": "",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                }
              ],
              "entityMap": [
                {
                  "key": "0",
                  "value": {
                    "data": {
                      "markdown": "```\n# Memory System\n\nYou have persistent memory. Use it.\n\n## Files\n- MEMORY.md — Long-term context. Read every session.\n- TOOLS.md — Technical configs and how-to.\n- memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md — Daily notes. Write immediately.\n- memory/active-tasks.md — In-progress work. Check on startup.\n- memory/lessons.md — Mistakes worth remembering.\n\n## Session Start\n1. Read MEMORY.md\n2. Check active-tasks.md — resume any interrupted work\n3. Read today's daily notes if they exist\n\n## During Session\nWrite to daily notes immediately when:\n- You complete something\n- You learn a preference\n- A decision gets made\n- Something important happens\n\n## Finding Things\nUse `qmd query \"search terms\"` to search across all memory files.\nUse `qmd get <file>:<line> -l N` to pull specific sections.\n\n## Maintenance\nPeriodically move important stuff from daily notes to MEMORY.md.\nMove technical learnings to TOOLS.md.\nRemove outdated content.\n\n## Principle\nText beats brain. If you want to remember it, write it down.\n```"
                    },
                    "mutability": "Mutable",
                    "type": "MARKDOWN"
                  }
                }
              ]
            },
            "media_entities": []
          },
          "twitter_card": "tweet",
          "color": null,
          "provider": "twitter"
        }
      }
    },

    "f6ab14d9b17acfaf083bb84b8b2348b5359d4c46b580bf7a654b554b0aca3940": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495083838,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/cathrynlavery/status/2024197229548839268",
          "id": "2024197229548839268",
          "text": "Openclaw tip 🦞\n\nI used to think my agent was broken when it went quiet after I messaged. Turns out it was just working on the task. \n\nFix: if a task takes >1 min, it must send “Got it” immediately, then follow with the result. Short tasks = normal reply. \n\nHuge UX win.",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "Openclaw tip 🦞\n\nI used to think my agent was broken when it went quiet after I messaged. Turns out it was just working on the task. \n\nFix: if a task takes &gt;1 min, it must send “Got it” immediately, then follow with the result. Short tasks = normal reply. \n\nHuge UX win. https://t.co/8mRzKltRJk",
            "facets": [
              {
                "type": "media",
                "indices": [
                  273,
                  296
                ],
                "id": "2024196831849115648",
                "display": "pic.x.com/8mRzKltRJk",
                "original": "https://t.co/8mRzKltRJk",
                "replacement": "https://x.com/cathrynlavery/status/2024197229548839268/photo/1"
              }
            ]
          },
          "author": {
            "id": "16904662",
            "name": "Cathryn",
            "screen_name": "cathrynlavery",
            "avatar_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1831441882892709888/xZ89wBfE_200x200.jpg",
            "banner_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_banners/16904662/1541605505",
            "description": "Founder @bestselfco ($55M+ bootstrapped). Sold to PE in 2022. Bought it back 2024. Documenting AI learnings @   Tweets about biz, ecom, AI.",
            "location": "Austin, TX",

    "f331a933c63a8fb937a97ea67e42c0f58760b3ebc76f5a33d6962393587e59d6": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495406447,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/pipelineclub100/status/2021433724831772821",
          "id": "2021433724831772821",
          "text": "What I’m beginning to understand about what agentic AI is capable of is going to change how we operate as an AE. \n\nImagine waking up to a Moltbot suggesting the strategy for your whole day? Setting up follow up emails in your own voice and writing style. Filling in your forecast and working to set next steps for all your opportunities. \n\nAnd AE who is skilled will be doing the work of 8 AEs.",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "What I’m beginning to understand about what agentic AI is capable of is going to change how we operate as an AE. \n\nImagine waking up to a Moltbot suggesting the strategy for your whole day? Setting up follow up emails in your own voice and writing style. Filling in your forecast and working to set next steps for all your opportunities. \n\nAnd AE who is skilled will be doing the work of 8 AEs.",
            "facets": []
          },

    "00bcec8e5d9717ccaac40e66b18f5c7fbae92c18775bd4e1390838eda12e2b91": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495407080,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/Saboo_Shubham_/status/2021416352637125110",
          "id": "2021416352637125110",
          "text": "",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "https://t.co/I00UtFrJdg",
            "facets": []
          },
          "author": {
            "id": "1275333333724000257",
            "name": "Shubham Saboo",
            "screen_name": "Saboo_Shubham_",
            "avatar_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2011150989668270080/wQ4NP39W_200x200.jpg",
            "banner_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_banners/1275333333724000257/1731204894",
            "description": "Senior AI Product Manager @google | Open Source Awesome LLM Apps Repo (#1 GitHub with 95k+ stars) | Author of books on GPT-3 & Neural Search | My views",
            "location": "AI Agents & RAG Tutorials →",
            "url": "https://x.com/Saboo_Shubham_",
            "followers": 105609,
            "following": 421,
            "joined": "Tue Jun 23 07:42:25 +0000 2020",
            "likes": 54693,
            "media_count": 3389,
            "protected": false,
            "website": {
              "url": "https://www.theunwindai.com",
              "display_url": "theunwindai.com"
            },
            "tweets": 18799,
            "avatar_color": null
          },
          "replies": 18,
          "retweets": 92,
          "likes": 650,
          "bookmarks": 1457,
          "created_at": "Wed Feb 11 02:49:49 +0000 2026",
          "created_timestamp": 1770778189,
          "possibly_sensitive": false,
          "views": 114205,
          "is_note_tweet": false,
          "community_note": null,
          "lang": null,
          "replying_to": null,
          "replying_to_status": null,
          "source": "Twitter Web App",
          "article": {
            "created_at": "2026-02-11T02:49:49.000Z",
            "modified_at": "2026-02-11T02:49:49.000Z",
            "id": "2021387318167470081",
            "title": "The Only Skills that Matter in 2026 ",
            "preview_text": "Six months ago, I would have hired for skills I now consider worthless.\nNot \"less important.\" Not \"evolving.\" Worthless. The skills that made developers valuable for the last decade got commoditized",
            "cover_media": {
              "id": "QVBJTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHA18Pcnb0AAKAAIRsuTo9tVwAQAA",
              "media_id": "2021408412580696064",
              "media_info": {
                "__typename": "ApiImage",
                "color_info": {
                  "palette": [
                    {
                      "percentage": 92,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 212,
                        "green": 238,
                        "red": 250
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 7.09,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 82,
                        "green": 116,
                        "red": 200
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 0.67,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 104,
                        "green": 143,
                        "red": 204
                      }
                    },
                    {
                      "percentage": 0.12,
                      "rgb": {
                        "blue": 108,
                        "green": 135,
                        "red": 197
                      }
                    }
                  ]
                },
                "original_img_height": 1306,
                "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HA18Pcnb0AA5CzP.jpg",
                "original_img_width": 3264
              },
              "media_key": "3_2021408412580696064"
            },
            "content": {
              "blocks": [
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "7qfkm",
                  "text": "Six months ago, I would have hired for skills I now consider worthless.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "9f1ls",
                  "text": "Not \"less important.\" Not \"evolving.\" Worthless. The skills that made developers valuable for the last decade got commoditized in one model generation. Not slowly. Not gradually. In the time between Gemini 3 pro and Claude Opus 4.6",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "f245c",
                  "text": "And most people haven't updated their mental model yet.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "44a60",
                  "text": "Meanwhile, the people shipping the fastest have almost entirely stopped writing code from scratch.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [
                    {
                      "key": 0,
                      "length": 16,
                      "offset": 64
                    }
                  ],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "411o9",
                  "text": "I've spent the last year building agents daily, maintaining the Awesome LLM Apps repo with 100+ open-source implementations, and shipping AI products at Google. I've watched the skills that matter shift in real time.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3td7u",
                  "text": "What got commoditized",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 26,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "2u9uf",
                  "text": "Writing code from scratch. Agents do it faster and with fewer bugs.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 28,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "20d62",
                  "text": "Boilerplate and scaffolding. One prompt. Done.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 27,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "2m28t",
                  "text": "Memorizing syntax and APIs. Context windows killed this.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 26,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "coqi6",
                  "text": "Translating specs to code. The spec IS the code now.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "bgkg6",
                  "text": "Agents do all of them now. Not always perfectly. Not without supervision. But fast enough that the bottleneck moved permanently upstream.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1mcr3",
                  "text": "An intern out-shipped a senior developer last week at a startup I advise. Not because the intern was better. Because they shaped the problem clearly and let Claude Code do the rest. The senior dev spent three days hand-writing what the intern shipped in an afternoon.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "9vaj9",
                  "text": "This isn't an anomaly. It's the new baseline.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "aeih7",
                  "text": "The skills above mattered because implementation was hard. They required years of practice. They justified six-figure salaries.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 44,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "o37k",
                  "text": "Implementation isn't the bottleneck anymore.  And most of the industry is still optimizing for the old bottleneck. The value shifted to five skills. Every person I know who's building effectively with agents has developed these, whether they name them or not.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "22v8k",
                  "text": "The new skillset",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 24,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "b1f99",
                  "text": "Skill 1: Problem shaping",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1j4ll",
                  "text": "Taking ambiguity and turning it into something an agent can act on. This is the skill that separates people who dabble with AI from people who build real products with it.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4s9it",
                  "text": "The skill is decomposition. Not \"build me a dashboard.\" That's a wish, not a task.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "7pnro",
                  "text": "Problem shaping turns that into twelve specific, testable sub-tasks with clear success criteria. What data does the dashboard show? What decisions does it inform? What should someone understand in the first three seconds?",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "16l0s",
                  "text": "When you break a vague goal into precise sub-problems, agents execute at a completely different level. Each sub-problem has clear inputs, clear outputs, clear success criteria. The agent doesn't have to guess what you mean.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1s3du",
                  "text": "Consider the C compiler fully built by Claude Code and Opus 4.6. One human shaped the problem. Sixteen agents executed. The result was 100,000 lines of working Rust. The human didn't write the code. They decomposed the problem so precisely that agents could build a compiler from the decomposition alone.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [
                    {
                      "key": 1,
                      "length": 1,
                      "offset": 0
                    }
                  ],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fuifl",
                  "text": " ",
                  "type": "atomic"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 102,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "buu91",
                  "text": "The leverage isn't in writing code. It's in breaking problems into pieces that agents can't get wrong.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3g739",
                  "text": "This is why problem shaping is now the highest-value skill. Not because it's new. PMs and senior engineers have always done this. But when the gap between \"well-shaped problem\" and \"shipped product\" collapses to hours, the quality of the problem shaping becomes the entire ballgame.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 25,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "asbll",
                  "text": "Skill 2: Context curation",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "7bka2",
                  "text": "The quality of what an agent produces is directly proportional to the context you feed it.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "eh96g",
                  "text": "Here's what bad context looks like:",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [
                    {
                      "key": 2,
                      "length": 1,
                      "offset": 0
                    }
                  ],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fjod6",
                  "text": " ",
                  "type": "atomic"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "55fh7",
                  "text": "Here's what good context looks like:",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [
                    {
                      "key": 3,
                      "length": 1,
                      "offset": 0
                    }
                  ],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "9sjni",
                  "text": " ",
                  "type": "atomic"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "a5m7f",
                  "text": "Same model. Same task. The first prompt gets you a generic chatbot. The second gets you something that feels like it was trained on your product for months.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 95,
                        "text": "CLAUDE.md",
                        "toIndex": 104
                      },
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 127,
                        "text": "GEMINI.md",
                        "toIndex": 136
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 80,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "88i3a",
                  "text": "The best builders I know spend more time writing context docs than writing code. They maintain CLAUDE.md files, .cursor/rules, GEMINI.md files that load automatically into every session. The agent starts every conversation already understanding their world.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "31g5v",
                  "text": "When I build a new agent for the Awesome LLM Apps repo, I never start from scratch. My context docs already know what \"good\" looks like, what patterns to use, what mistakes to avoid. First output is 90% there instead of 50%.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3e07d",
                  "text": "That 40% gap is the difference between \"technically works\" and \"actually ships.\" And it comes entirely from context, not from coding ability.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 14,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "lh2t",
                  "text": "Skill 3: Taste",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "8j3p6",
                  "text": "Taste means knowing what to build before it exists. Knowing what \"good\" looks like when you're staring at ten options and nine of them are wrong.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "c349m",
                  "text": "Last week I asked Antigravity to build a new agent for the Awesome LLM Apps repo. An AI negotiation battle simulator where two agents haggle over a used car, buyer versus seller, with distinct personalities and live streaming via AG-UI.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "d7ghh",
                  "text": "The first version ran perfectly. Clean code. No errors. Agents negotiated back and forth. Technically complete.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4b3tq",
                  "text": "I rejected it in thirty seconds.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "f4ct",
                  "text": "The UI was a plain chat window. The negotiation felt like reading a log file. There were no personalities, no tension, no moment where you're watching a \"Shark Steve\" hold firm against a \"Cool-Hand Casey\" bluffing a walkaway. It worked as software. It failed as an experience.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4p1bs",
                  "text": "With 93k+ stars on the repo, I've seen how developers actually engage with agent demos. They decide in under ten minutes if something is worth their time. A working agent isn't enough. It has to feel like something you want to show someone.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 84,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "bda95",
                  "text": "An agent can build anything you describe. It can't tell you what's worth describing. The agent optimized for correctness. I optimized for \"would someone actually clone this?\"",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "98s3c",
                  "text": "That's taste. Not design sense in the abstract. The accumulated judgment of what makes someone star a repo versus close the tab.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "bhe97",
                  "text": "When agents produce output quickly and in bulk, the person who can spot which version actually lands is the most valuable person in the room. This is harder to develop than it sounds. You can't read a book about taste. You build it by shipping things, watching what users actually do, and developing an instinct for the gap between \"works\" and \"worth using.\"",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "7o676",
                  "text": "One exercise that helps: review your last five agent outputs. For each one, write down what you'd change and why. That \"why\" is taste developing.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 28,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "bl8lq",
                  "text": "Skill 4: Agent orchestration",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "clahi",
                  "text": "Knowing when to use one agent versus many. When to run parallel versus sequential. When to add guardrails versus letting it run. When to step in and debug manually versus letting the agent work through it.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "72kp7",
                  "text": "That last one matters more than people think. Agents are good at tracing errors, but they can also get stuck in loops, retrying the same failed approach over and over. Knowing when to let the agent debug and when to step in yourself is a real skill. Blind trust in agent debugging is as costly as doing it all manually.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "8kn6m",
                  "text": "The three patterns that matter:",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 20,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "6uo0g",
                  "text": "Sequential pipeline. Agent A finishes, passes output to Agent B. Simple. Predictable. Use this when each step depends on the previous one. A research agent gathers data, then an analysis agent interprets it, then a writing agent drafts the report.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 34,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "6s5qf",
                  "text": "Coordinator with specialized team. One lead agent delegates to specialists and synthesizes their work. Use this when you need quality control. The coordinator catches when a specialist goes off track, re-prompts, and merges the results. This is how I build most complex workflows now.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 30,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "45ev8",
                  "text": "Parallel execution with merge. Multiple agents run simultaneously on independent tasks, then a final agent combines the outputs. Use this when sub-tasks don't depend on each other. Run market research, competitor analysis, and user interviews in parallel. Merge at the end. What used to take a sequential afternoon now takes minutes.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "458gs",
                  "text": "Most people default to sequential because it feels safe. But knowing when to parallelize and when to add a coordinator is the difference between an agent workflow that takes five minutes and one that takes an hour.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 74,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "3325e",
                  "text": "The best developers in 2026 are more like film directors than programmers. They set the scene, cast the agents, and know when to yell cut. They don't write every line. They shape the performance.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "2eud3",
                  "text": "You learn this by building. Not by reading about it.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 55,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "581h6",
                  "text": "Skill 5: Knowing when NOT to use Agents (aka Judgement)",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "et0t2",
                  "text": "This sounds contradictory in an article about agent-era skills. But the trap is real. When Claude Code can build anything you describe, you start describing everything to Claude Code.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "d42qr",
                  "text": "I catch myself reaching for agents for problems that don't need an agent at all. A quick UI tweak. A config change. A simple refactor. The agent overhead, the context loading, the prompt crafting, the output evaluation, sometimes exceeds the value of the task itself.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1qvs8",
                  "text": "Not every problem needs an agent. Some problems need a fast model and a clear prompt.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "8s4qe",
                  "text": "Need to reformat a JSON file? Throw it at Gemini 3 Flash. Simple copy change across ten files? A quick prompt to any lightweight model handles it in seconds. Bug that you already understand? Faster to fix it yourself than to explain it to an agent.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [
                    {
                      "length": 46,
                      "offset": 0,
                      "style": "Bold"
                    }
                  ],
                  "key": "1ird6",
                  "text": "The skill is matching the tool to the problem. Agents for ambiguous, multi-step work where you need to explore the solution space. Fast models for simple, well-defined tasks where you already know the answer. Your own hands for the things you can type faster than you can describe.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "rkff",
                  "text": "The people who are most effective aren't the ones who use agents for everything. They're the ones who reach for the right tool at the right level. Agent when the problem is hard. Model when the problem is simple. Keyboard when the problem is obvious.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "96fst",
                  "text": "How to build these skills",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "dvus9",
                  "text": "You can't develop any of these by reading about them. You develop them by building.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "94lv3",
                  "text": "Taste: Review your last 5 agent outputs. Write down what you'd change and why.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {
                    "urls": [
                      {
                        "fromIndex": 17,
                        "text": "CLAUDE.md",
                        "toIndex": 26
                      }
                    ]
                  },
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1ud54",
                  "text": "Context: Write a CLAUDE.md for your current project. Spend 30 minutes.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "878gc",
                  "text": "Problem shaping: Take your next vague request and decompose it into 10 sub-tasks before prompting.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1cdi9",
                  "text": "Orchestration: Take a sequential workflow and identify which steps could run in parallel.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "1vgmn",
                  "text": "Knowing your tools: Track for one week when you used an agent for something a simple prompt would have handled.",
                  "type": "unordered-list-item"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "du8it",
                  "text": "Pick one. Do it today. The intuition builds faster than you think.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "emk5i",
                  "text": "What's actually left",
                  "type": "header-two"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3sqg3",
                  "text": "The skills that mattered for twenty years mattered because implementation was hard. Implementation isn't hard anymore.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "3qo4b",
                  "text": "What's left is everything upstream. Taste. Context curation. Problem decomposition. Agent orchestration. And the judgment to know when to reach for an agent, when a simple model prompt is enough, and when to use your own hands.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "4qm3v",
                  "text": "These aren't new skills exactly. The best engineers and PMs always had them. But they used to be buried under layers of implementation work. Now they're exposed. Now they're the whole job.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "f2mj9",
                  "text": "Open your last project. Ask yourself: did you spend more time writing code or shaping the problem? If the answer is code, you're building with the old skillset.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                },
                {
                  "data": {},
                  "entityRanges": [],
                  "inlineStyleRanges": [],
                  "key": "fdt0i",
                  "text": "The new one starts with a context doc and a clear problem statement. The code writes itself.",
                  "type": "unstyled"
                }
              ],
              "entityMap": [
                {
                  "key": "0",
                  "value": {
                    "data": {
                      "url": "https://github.com/Shubhamsaboo/awesome-llm-apps"
                    },
                    "mutability": "Mutable",
                    "type": "LINK"
                  }
                },
                {
                  "key": "1",
                  "value": {
                    "data": {
                      "tweetId": "2019496582698397945"
                    },
                    "mutability": "Immutable",
                    "type": "TWEET"
                  }
                },
                {
                  "key": "2",
                  "value": {
                    "data": {
                      "markdown": "```markdown\nBuild me a customer support agent.\n```"
                    },
                    "mutability": "Mutable",
                    "type": "MARKDOWN"
                  }
                },
                {
                  "key": "3",
                  "value": {
                    "data": {
                      "markdown": "```markdown\nTarget user: SaaS customers who are frustrated and considering canceling.\nThey've already tried the help docs. They're messaging because docs failed them.\n\nTone: Empathetic but efficient. Don't over-apologize. Don't be robotic.\nHere are 3 real tickets that got 5-star ratings: [examples]\nHere are 2 that got complaints: [examples]\n\nEdge cases requiring human handoff:\n- Billing disputes over $500\n- Account security concerns\n- Legal or compliance questions\n\nSuccess metric: Resolution without escalation in under 4 messages.\n```"
                    },
                    "mutability": "Mutable",
                    "type": "MARKDOWN"
                  }
                }
              ]
            },
            "media_entities": []
          },
          "twitter_card": "tweet",
          "color": null,
          "provider": "twitter"
        }
      }
    },

    "4e476f2142db2e3443c1973760f8d62fdc030a522ddc08e601025cd6ffb88763": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495408524,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/BenyaminHolley/status/2021230916400447985",
          "id": "2021230916400447985",
          "text": "I get this question probably once a day now. \"Should I use Claude Code or Clay for this?\" It's a good question and it makes sense why everyone's asking it right now with all the Claude Code hype. But I think it's the wrong question.\n\nInstead of asking which tool, ask yourself three things about the workflow you're building:\n\n- Does it need to run without you running it? \n- Do you want your team to be able to build on it? \n- Does it need to be institutionalized, visible, and producing quantifiable results over time?\n\nIf the answer to any of those is yes, build a Clay table. \nSet it on a schedule. \nLet it run.\n\nYou set up a Clay table that monitors an RSS feed of podcasts in your space, reads the transcripts, identifies the guests, and sends them a personalized email based on what they actually talked about on the show. It needs to run without you, forever, and Clay is purpose-built for that.\n\nNow let's say you go to a trade show and come back with a list of attendees. You need to enrich them, write custom copy, and ship a single outbound campaign, and upload the scrubbed/enriched list to Salesforce and build a report/campaign around it for ROI tracking. That's a one-time project. You're not running it again next week. Claude Code handles that perfectly. You describe what you want, it builds it, you execute, you move on. That's more of a manual revops function that, even if you do it again next month, might entail a different process. \n\nSo right now, the distinction is pretty clear. Clay is for recurring infrastructure. Claude Code is for one-off projects with heavy context.\n\nBut that line is starting to blur. What I'm exploring right now is having Claude Code build a workflow and then deploy it as a cron job that runs on a schedule of some kind inside something like n8n or trigger (dot) dev. No manual re-runs. No babysitting. Once that path is reliable, you're basically having Claude Code build its own version of a Clay table. And at that point, the gap between these two tools gets very small.\n\nThe obvious issue with this is that Clay has a lot more benefits than just running tasks on schedule, not sure how I'm going to mitigate those but I feel like I'm really close to being able to get out of Clay entirely as towards the tail end of my time at Cyft it became one of the most expensive pieces of my stack. \n\nThe main issue of course is you're now working with a vibe coded SaaS and we all know the issues there, but the other big one, is that Clays UI, despite having issues, is easy and makes sense to normies. Not sure how we're going to solve for that if I end up making a bunch of recurring jobs like this. I suppose you could just tell Claude Code to remake Clays front end or something.",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "I get this question probably once a day now. \"Should I use Claude Code or Clay for this?\" It's a good question and it makes sense why everyone's asking it right now with all the Claude Code hype. But I think it's the wrong question.\n\nInstead of asking which tool, ask yourself three things about the workflow you're building:\n\n- Does it need to run without you running it? \n- Do you want your team to be able to build on it? \n- Does it need to be institutionalized, visible, and producing quantifiable results over time?\n\nIf the answer to any of those is yes, build a Clay table. \nSet it on a schedule. \nLet it run.\n\nYou set up a Clay table that monitors an RSS feed of podcasts in your space, reads the transcripts, identifies the guests, and sends them a personalized email based on what they actually talked about on the show. It needs to run without you, forever, and Clay is purpose-built for that.\n\nNow let's say you go to a trade show and come back with a list of attendees. You need to enrich them, write custom copy, and ship a single outbound campaign, and upload the scrubbed/enriched list to Salesforce and build a report/campaign around it for ROI tracking. That's a one-time project. You're not running it again next week. Claude Code handles that perfectly. You describe what you want, it builds it, you execute, you move on. That's more of a manual revops function that, even if you do it again next month, might entail a different process. \n\nSo right now, the distinction is pretty clear. Clay is for recurring infrastructure. Claude Code is for one-off projects with heavy context.\n\nBut that line is starting to blur. What I'm exploring right now is having Claude Code build a workflow and then deploy it as a cron job that runs on a schedule of some kind inside something like n8n or trigger (dot) dev. No manual re-runs. No babysitting. Once that path is reliable, you're basically having Claude Code build its own version of a Clay table. And at that point, the gap between these two tools gets very small.\n\nThe obvious issue with this is that Clay has a lot more benefits than just running tasks on schedule, not sure how I'm going to mitigate those but I feel like I'm really close to being able to get out of Clay entirely as towards the tail end of my time at Cyft it became one of the most expensive pieces of my stack. \n\nThe main issue of course is you're now working with a vibe coded SaaS and we all know the issues there, but the other big one, is that Clays UI, despite having issues, is easy and makes sense to normies. Not sure how we're going to solve for that if I end up making a bunch of recurring jobs like this. I suppose you could just tell Claude Code to remake Clays front end or something.",
            "facets": []
          },
          "author": {
            "id": "1465416677210927110",
            "name": "🏍benyamin",
            "screen_name": "BenyaminHolley",
            "avatar_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2016996264294977536/GyJ6sLI__200x200.jpg",
            "banner_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_banners/1465416677210927110/1769723134",
            "description": "gtme at @AirOpsHQ | 2016 Honda VFR1200X | taking big swings",
            "location": "Charlotte NC",

    "a03c9d6de82c60312bedd9cfab0824b983d6ca8382c3ab3292447e5a2e834925": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495409183,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/jerryjliu0/status/2021021110721265979",
          "id": "2021021110721265979",
          "text": "We built LobsterX 🦞, an @openclaw specialized for document work on your computer.\n\nIt uses high-accuracy document parsing, extraction, classification through LlamaCloud, meaning it can comb through complicated PDFs (with scans, tables, diagrams) and extract out 100% accurate context!\n\nIt can run as a Telegram bot and is built on top of agentfs (@tursodatabase) as a file system.\n\nBig shoutout to @itsclelia. This is a fun project inspired by @openclaw’s success, and besides being a fun tool to use, it can be a great reference for building your own generalized coding agents!\n\nReadme: https://github.com/AstraBert/workflows-acp/blob/main/packages/lobsterx/README.md\nLlamaCloud: https://cloud.llamaindex.ai/signup",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "We built LobsterX 🦞, an @openclaw specialized for document work on your computer.\n\nIt uses high-accuracy document parsing, extraction, classification through LlamaCloud, meaning it can comb through complicated PDFs (with scans, tables, diagrams) and extract out 100% accurate context!\n\nIt can run as a Telegram bot and is built on top of agentfs (@tursodatabase) as a file system.\n\nBig shoutout to @itsclelia. This is a fun project inspired by @openclaw’s success, and besides being a fun tool to use, it can be a great reference for building your own generalized coding agents!\n\nReadme: https://t.co/ywtmASOoO4\nLlamaCloud: https://t.co/qsGFaIL7uM",
            "facets": [
              {

          },
          "quote": {
            "url": "https://x.com/itsclelia/status/2020913910686355709",
            "id": "2020913910686355709",
            "text": "The tech world went crazy for @openclaw, so I decided to build a similar crustacean agent called LobsterX🦞, with a focus on document-processing tasks.\nI wrote a blog about it: http://clelia.dev/2026-02-09-the-anatomy-of-a-document-processing-agent, but, as a tl;dr:\n- LobsterX uses @llama_index cloud products to parse, extract structured data and classify files\n- The agent has only access to a virtual filesystem (AgentFS by @tursodatabase) to avoid damaging your real one, and cannot execute arbitrary bash commands, preventing it from performing dangerous or security-critical operations🔒\n- The agent is self-hostable and can be used both as a @Docker image and as a uv tool, and is available as a @telegram bot💬\n\n📦 Install: 𝘶𝘷 𝘵𝘰𝘰𝘭 𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘭𝘭 𝘭𝘰𝘣𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘳𝘹 --𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘳𝘦𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘦=𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘰𝘸\n📚 Read more: http://github.com/AstraBert/workflows-acp/blob/main/packages/lobsterx/README.md\n📝 Read the blog: http://clelia.dev/2026-02-09-the-anatomy-of-a-document-processing-agent",
            "raw_text": {
              "text": "The tech world went crazy for @openclaw, so I decided to build a similar crustacean agent called LobsterX🦞, with a focus on document-processing tasks.\nI wrote a blog about it: https://t.co/x3ABScSQLC, but, as a tl;dr:\n- LobsterX uses @llama_index cloud products to parse, extract structured data and classify files\n- The agent has only access to a virtual filesystem (AgentFS by @tursodatabase) to avoid damaging your real one, and cannot execute arbitrary bash commands, preventing it from performing dangerous or security-critical operations🔒\n- The agent is self-hostable and can be used both as a @Docker image and as a uv tool, and is available as a @telegram bot💬\n\n📦 Install: 𝘶𝘷 𝘵𝘰𝘰𝘭 𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘭𝘭 𝘭𝘰𝘣𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘳𝘹 --𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘳𝘦𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘦=𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘰𝘸\n📚 Read more: https://t.co/rg9sxOt9ey\n📝 Read the blog: https://t.co/x3ABScSQLC",
              "facets": [
                {

              "banner_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_banners/1925178481534509056/1752512905",
              "description": "she/her |\nOpen Sourceress at @llama_index 🦙 | \nGirly dev building AI things in python💅 | Cheating on python with Go and Rust🦫🦀",
              "location": "",

              "all": [
                {

    "518c6f2e36ddeb0ff1e4da9a993d684fca986bb39caea6e74bcb7bb324a464c5": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495409906,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/stevekaliski/status/2021034048945070360",
          "id": "2021034048945070360",
          "text": "At Stripe we have a tool called \"minions\" -- it lets us kick off async agents built right in our dev environment to one-shot bugs, features, and more e2e.\n\nI have team, project, and personal channels dedicated just to working with minions.\n\nI like to think of it as a new type of pair programming -- \"pair prompting.\"\n\nRead more --> https://stripe.dev/blog/minions-stripes-one-shot-end-to-end-coding-agents",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "At Stripe we have a tool called \"minions\" -- it lets us kick off async agents built right in our dev environment to one-shot bugs, features, and more e2e.\n\nI have team, project, and personal channels dedicated just to working with minions.\n\nI like to think of it as a new type of pair programming -- \"pair prompting.\"\n\nRead more --> https://t.co/0A6vDEOEjL",
            "facets": [
              {
                "type": "url",
                "indices": [
                  333,
                  356
                ],
                "original": "https://t.co/0A6vDEOEjL",
                "replacement": "https://stripe.dev/blog/minions-stripes-one-shot-end-to-end-coding-agents",
                "display": "stripe.dev/blog/minions-s…"
              }
            ]
          },

    "ce3e561b352394e4403efb998f99947ff42fe2146ac901d416bf0861ab7d5767": {
      "expiresAtEpochMs": 1771495410535,
      "value": {
        "code": 200,
        "message": "OK",
        "tweet": {
          "url": "https://x.com/crystalsssup/status/2021128641917354475",
          "id": "2021128641917354475",
          "text": "If you need McKinsey-style slides, try this prompt:\n\nI asked Kimi to conduct a comprehensive analysis of the GenAI video model market, focusing on leading players (e.g., Seedance 2.0, Sora, Kling, Veo, Luma).\n\nMy prompt:\nConduct a comprehensive analysis of the GenAI video model market, focusing on leading players (e.g., Seedance 2.0, Kling, Veo, Luma). Compare their core architectures, temporal consistency, and prompt adherence to identify current industry benchmarks. Use the latest information\n\nRequirement:\nA professional, high-density consulting presentation slide, designed in the style of a top-tier strategy firm (McKinsey/BCG) blended with high-end editorial aesthetics.\n\nCore Content & Layout:\n1. Rich Data Visualization: The slide is populated with complex, precise charts (stacked bar charts, waterfall charts, or line graphs) and detailed data tables with rows and columns.\n2. Structured Frameworks: Includes strategic diagrams or 2x2 matrices constructed with thin, clean lines.\n3. High Information Density: The layout is sophisticated and multi-column, mimicking an actual business analysis deck, not just an empty cover page.\n\nVisual Style:\n1. Aesthetic: Tech-minimalist but information-heavy. Clean, sharp, and authoritative.\n2. Typography: Serif fonts (like Times New Roman) for the main headlines to give a premium financial report feel; clean Sans-serif for chart labels and data numbers.\n3. Color Palette: Clean white background. Text is sharp black. Charts and graphical accents use Deep Royal Blue and distinct shades of grey for data hierarchy.\n4. Graphics: Use fine hairline borders for tables and precise vector lines for graphs.",
          "raw_text": {
            "text": "If you need McKinsey-style slides, try this prompt:\n\nI asked Kimi to conduct a comprehensive analysis of the GenAI video model market, focusing on leading players (e.g., Seedance 2.0, Sora, Kling, Veo, Luma).\n\nMy prompt:\nConduct a comprehensive analysis of the GenAI video model market, focusing on leading players (e.g., Seedance 2.0, Kling, Veo, Luma). Compare their core architectures, temporal consistency, and prompt adherence to identify current industry benchmarks. Use the latest information\n\nRequirement:\nA professional, high-density consulting presentation slide, designed in the style of a top-tier strategy firm (McKinsey/BCG) blended with high-end editorial aesthetics.\n\nCore Content & Layout:\n1. Rich Data Visualization: The slide is populated with complex, precise charts (stacked bar charts, waterfall charts, or line graphs) and detailed data tables with rows and columns.\n2. Structured Frameworks: Includes strategic diagrams or 2x2 matrices constructed with thin, clean lines.\n3. High Information Density: The layout is sophisticated and multi-column, mimicking an actual business analysis deck, not just an empty cover page.\n\nVisual Style:\n1. Aesthetic: Tech-minimalist but information-heavy. Clean, sharp, and authoritative.\n2. Typography: Serif fonts (like Times New Roman) for the main headlines to give a premium financial report feel; clean Sans-serif for chart labels and data numbers.\n3. Color Palette: Clean white background. Text is sharp black. Charts and graphical accents use Deep Royal Blue and distinct shades of grey for data hierarchy.\n4. Graphics: Use fine hairline borders for tables and precise vector lines for graphs.",

          "id": "2020863877068603661",
          "text": "Holy shit  This is fucking insane\n\nSeedance 2.0 can actually make motion graphics and APP promo videos too!  \n\nThis is GPT-4o image level of moment for video models , from here it will go high only \n\nThanks to chinese creator for this video",

            "title": "the \"living files\" theory",
            "preview_text": "most people think they're organized.\nthey have their Google Drive. their Notion. their Obsidian vault. folders inside of folders inside of folders.\nand they feel productive because everything is \"in",
